docs.title: What is it?
docs.text: Mango Framework is my take on creating a professional and feature full, but still easy to use, framework to handle all scripts dor creating experiences on Roblox. While it might be similar to others, this framework has it's own style. The layout in the framework is straight forward and easy to learn. It's been revised over multiple years to handle anything you might need in a game. Depending on the branch you choose, it's functionality can range from only the essentials to a full set of integrated tools for speeding up development.

docs.title: Faq
docs.text: No questions?!

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A folder used for storing client-sided module scripts. The modules from this folder are automatically required and initialized by the client runtime.

docs.title: Controller Structure Template
docs.text:
Feel free to copy this template to use in your controllers, or add it to your snippets in VSC for easy boilerplate insertion. 

local Controller = {}

--[=[
    The construction method is the first method that is called
    immediately after the controller has been required by the
    runtime script.

    This method is intended to wrap the whole scope of the 
    controller so try to avoid placing anything outside of it.

    This wrapping is done to prevent the possibility of
    recursive requiring of modules from throwing errors.

    The method name can be redefined in the RuntimeSettings
    module located at ReplicatedStorage.Settings.RuntimeSettings
]=]--
function Controller.__Construct()

    --[=[
        This is the initialization function, it's purpose is to handle any
        code that might require dependencies that couldn't be loaded
        in the construction method call.

        It is called only when all other controllers have been constructed.

        I found only rare cases when this is actually necessary, but in case
        those cases appear it's there.

        Locally you can call it whatever, but the actual exported name of
        the function can be changed in the RuntimeSettings.
    ]=]--
    local function Init()

    end

    --[=[
        Exporting the init script.
    ]=]--
    Controller.__Init = Init
end

return Controller

docs.title: Description
docs.text: This is the main point of interaction for your client modules. It's the module manager that requires and caches modules dependencies. This is the only module you will actually call require() on since all the other modules are cached by it and accessible with a method. It also ensures, to a certain extent, that modules dependencies are constructed before the module. It does this by constructing the dependency modules itself, making it a kind of automatic priority system.

docs.title: Adding a dependency
docs.text:
If you want to add a dependency (ie. add access to controller B from controller A) you can call within the construct method of a controller: 

-- Controller A:
local MangoClient = require(ReplicatedStorage.MangoClient)
local ControllerB = MangoClient.Require("ControllerB")

docs.title: .Require()
docs.text:
Used for requiring dependencies from controllers. 

MangoClient.Require(moduleName: string): (string) -> any

docs.title: .LockModule()
docs.text:
Prevents the module from being accessed again by .Require() making it's scope safe from outside calls. 

MangoClient.LockModule(moduleName: string): (string) -> nil

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Values up for configuration (located at the top of MangoClient):

- MODULE_WAIT_TIMEOUT: number -- Time that MangoClient will wait for a module before declaring it missing.
- MODULE_CONTAINERS: { [number] = Instance } -- Every container that MangoClient will grab top-level modules from, it will never grab descendants of those modules.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A folder storing pre-packaged controllers available in some branches of Mango Framework. These controllers are wrappers for common functions widely usable in any type of experience. They are general purpose controllers that you can use to save time while working on other, more important, systems.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A folder storing pre-packaged resources available in some branches of Mango Framework. The resources included are some that I have encountered and used the most. If you don't want these resources included in your download, consider the Barebones Branch of the framework.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
The resources folder is a container for all modules, components and utils modules you need. It is separated into two sub-folders which divide the client modules from the modules shared between both the client and the server.

These modules are not automatically required by the framework, which also means that their structure is entirely up to you.

Additionally, there is another division within both of those sub-folders. In their main directory you can store modules you created. There is also a folder named External which is reserved for modules that you import into your project.

It is up to you if you want to follow this structure, it is just something I recommend. The External folder doesn't need to be populated, nor even exist, for the framework to still work.

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
This is a folder which contains all your settings / config files. They are available to both the client and the server.

You can bunch them up or, as I like to, name them based on their relation to controller-service systems (RoundService + RoundController -> RoundSettings)

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Ideally the settings files should contain to actual code, only field values. By this the standard structure of a settings file should just be: 

return {
    SOME_KEY = "SOME_VALUE"
}

docs.title: MangoResources
docs.text: Shares the functionality with MangoResources in ReplicatedStorage. Though it only contains resources available on the server.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The core functionality of MangoServer is similar to MangoClient, though there are additional functions that MangoServer introduces.

docs.title: Adding a dependency
docs.text:
The call to get a dependency exactly matches the call you make to the MangoClient manager: 

-- Service A:
local ServerStorage = game:GetService("ServerStorage")
local MangoServer = require(ServerStorage.MangoServer)
local ServiceB = MangoServer.Require("ServiceB")

docs.title: Caching for Players
docs.text:
MangoServer adds an internally managed field of values, created for each player. This prevents the need of creating cache tables in each service to store player associated values. 

MangoServer.SetCacheField("timesJumped", 42)
warn(MangoServer.GetCacheField("timesJumped")) --> 42

docs.title: General Cache Handlers
docs.text:
There are additional sub-handlers included in the manager. It allows for applying general cache field values automatically as soon as the game starts. These values are intended to be universally used. They exist so no one service is responsible for creating globally accessible values. (for example, a value for the time a player joined)

More documentation can be found in the TEMPLATE.luau file provided in the handler container.

docs.title: .Require()
docs.text:
Used for requiring dependencies from services. 

MangoServer.Require(moduleName: string): (string) -> any

docs.title: .SetCacheField()
docs.text:
Caches a value for an internally managed field of values, created for each player. 

MangoServer.SetCacheField(fieldName: string, newValue: any?): (string, any?) -> nil

docs.title: .GetCacheField()
docs.text:
Fetches the value of a field item in the previously mentioned field of cached per-player values. 

MangoServer.GetCacheField(fieldName: string): (string) -> any?

docs.title: MangoServices
docs.text: In function, the same as MangoControllers. Instead of containing controllers, it contains services.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text: Structured the same as Resources in ReplicatedStorage. It's different in that it contains modules only available to the server.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Similar to Controllers, it's a folder used for storing server-sided module scripts. The modules from this folder are automatically required and initialized by the server runtime.

docs.title: Service Structure Template
docs.text:
You can copy this and use it as a template, though you don't have to. Try to follow the outlined structure for the framework to work as indented. 

local Service = {}

--[=[
    The construction method is the first method that is called
    immediately after the service has been required by the
    runtime script.

    This method is intended to wrap the whole scope of the 
    service so try to avoid placing anything outside of it.

    This wrapping is done to prevent the possibility of
    recursive requiring of modules from throwing errors.

    The method name can be redefined in the RuntimeSettings
    module located at ReplicatedStorage.Settings.RuntimeSettings
]=]--
function Service.__Construct()

    --[=[
        This is the initialization function, it's purpose is to handle any
        code that might require dependencies that couldn't be loaded
        in the construction method call.

        It is called only when all other services have been constructed.

        I found only rare cases when this is actually necessary, but in case
        those cases appear it's there.

        Locally you can call it whatever, but the actual exported name of
        the function can be changed in the RuntimeSettings.
    ]=]--
    local function Init()

    end

    --[=[
        Exporting the init script.
    ]=]--
    Service.__Init = Init
end

return Service

docs.title: About Features
docs.text: Theres tons of useful features in Mango Framework. All of them have, at one time, been used in practice. Features are constantly developing and improving. Suggestions for additional features are open.

docs.title: Behavior Features
docs.text:
• One-script runtime.
  • Sequential module loading.
  • Server loading priority (server always loads fully first)

docs.title: Module Management Features
docs.text:
• Both server and client sided module loading.
  • Dependency and priority management.
  • Caching of modules to a quickly accessible location.
  • Handling of modules with unexpected failures.
  • Prevention of cyclic dependencies.
  • Infinite modular expandability.
  • Extensive debugging information.

docs.title: Organization Features
docs.text:
• Semantic module storage.
  • Proprietary standardized layout.

docs.title: Data Accessibility Features
docs.text:
• Faster global value storage.
  • Per-player cache management.

docs.title: About
docs.text: On the other hand, if you prefer working in the Roblox script editor directly you can copy the already built place containing Mango Framework

docs.title: Downloading
docs.text:
1. Select a branch:

    • Barebones Branch

  2. Open the experience options.

  3. Download the place.

After downloading the place file, you can open it and publish it under your name. The file should contain the whole framework with the branch of your choosing ready to go.

docs.title: About
docs.text: Installing Mango Framework from source gives you the greatest amount of flexibility with the system. This is how it was developed and used by myself. To visit the framework source code page click here

docs.title: Directly from Github
docs.text:
1. Choosing a branch Choosing a branch lets you pick what features will come with your download of Mango Framework. Each branch has a different set of branch features.

  2. Press the Code button in the top right of the repository.

  3. Press "Download ZIP" button, which will download the whole repository as a zipped archive.

  4. Create a folder, anywhere, that will be your work folder.

  5. Unzip the archive into you new work folder.

docs.title: With Git
docs.text:
1. Open a terminal
  2. Move into the directory that will be the parent directory of your workspace folder.
  3. Choose a branch from the Github repository.
  4. Run: 
    git clone -b <BRANCH_NAME> https://github.com/MarkoGasta/Mango-Framework.git

docs.title: Opening your workspace
docs.text: After downloading the repository, you can open it Visual Studio Code or your IDE of choice. The workspace should be Rojo ready and work off the bat.

docs.title: Limits
docs.text:
Mango Framework makes connection modules really simple. As long as the modules you are connection are within the same scope:

  • Client -> Client
  • Server -> Server
  • Client & Server -> Shared

docs.title: Client
docs.text:
To find where the manager is pulling modules from, you can check out the configuration section on it's docs page.

-- ControllerA:
local ReplicatedSTorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local MangoClient = require(ReplicatedStorage.MangoClient)
local ControllerB = MangoClient.Require("ControllerB")
local Utils = MangoClient.Require("Utils")

docs.title: Server
docs.text:
-- ServiceA:
local ServerStorage = game:GetService("ServerStorage")
local MangoServer = require(ServerStorage.MangoServer)
local ServiceB = MangoServer.Require("ServiceB")
local Utils = MangoServer.Require("Utils")

docs.title: Where?
docs.text: Controller are located in the src/ReplicatedStorage/Controllers folder.

docs.title: How?
docs.text:
1. Create a ModuleScript.
  2. Name it for whatever you need.
  3. Add the necessary functions.
  4. Thats it!

docs.title: Where?
docs.text: Services are located in the src/ServerStorage/Services folder.

docs.title: How?
docs.text:
1. Create a ModuleScript.
  2. Name it for whatever you need.
  3. Add the necessary functions.
  4. Thats it!

docs.title: Top Level Layout
docs.text:
The top level layout of Mango Framework takes inspiration from Roblox services. Each script is a descendant of the folder you can find in studio.

graph LR
    src[src] --> rs[ReplicatedStorage]
    src --> sss[ServerScriptService]
    src --> ss[ServerStorage]
    src --> sps[StarterPlayerService]
    sps --> spls[StarterPlayerScripts]
    sps --> schs[StarterCharacterScripts]

docs.title: Managing Priorities
docs.text:
Priorities of controllers and services can be changed. This means you can change the order in which they get constructed and initialized. This allows you to, with even higher precision, control which modules have access to what code.

The priorities of modules can be set in the src/ReplicatedStorage/Settings/RuntimeSettings.luau file.

There are two fields in this file:

CONTROLLER_PRIORITIES: { [string]: number }
SERVICE_PRIORITIES: { [string]: number }

Each field is a dictionary which has the module name as the key and the priority as the value. Next to those, there is also a field which holds the value all modules will default to if not specified.

DEFAULT_PRIORITY: number
